query: React one way data flow interview question

competitors.label: Sharing State Between Components
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React page for single source of truth and shared state ownership, which is the closest official baseline for one-way data flow.
competitors.winReason: The official docs teach single-source-of-truth mechanics through UI examples, but our page is stronger on multi-writer production bugs, implicit two-way coupling, and why React's rendering model depends on explicit ownership.
theirStrengths: It is authoritative on shared state, controlled components, and single source of truth.
theirGaps: It is more tutorial-shaped than production-debug shaped.

realWorldUseCases: That discipline prevents multi-writer bugs, makes updates traceable in production, and keeps React’s render/reconciliation model safe to restart or interrupt.
followUpConfusion: Two-way binding often means multiple places can write to the same piece of data (child + parent, or UI + model).

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain single source of truth through shared accordion state, but they are less explicit about multi-writer production failures.
actionableExamples: They include a full working example of lifting state and coordinating sibling panels.
followUpConfusion: The page explains state ownership well, but our answer more directly names prop mutation and shared mutable objects as the follow-up failure mode.

actionableExamples: Add one short before-and-after example where duplicated child state drifts and lifting it fixes the bug.

competitors.label: Thinking Declaratively in React Interviews
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's declarative React playbook is directly relevant because one-way data flow is usually taught through state ownership and declarative updates in interviews.
competitors.winReason: The declarative playbook is still useful, but our page is now more concrete on the exact failure mode one-way flow prevents: sibling drift, shared mutable data, and the distinction between one-way ownership and ordinary prop drilling.
theirStrengths: It is interview-oriented and frames React through declarative state-driven UI rules.
theirGaps: It is broader than this one concept and therefore less compressed around one-way data flow specifically.

realWorldUseCases: If the preview child keeps its own local copy while the form child also writes to a different copy, the UI drifts: one part of the screen shows stale text, and save submits something else.
followUpConfusion: One-way flow is not the same as no prop drilling.

realWorldUseCases: The playbook relates declarative React to predictable UI state, so the real-world lens is close.
actionableExamples: It provides more interview-oriented framing around declarative state patterns than our shorter answer.
followUpConfusion: Both pages address why state should have a single owner and why children should not push data sideways.

realWorldUseCases: Add one bug example where sibling components both try to own the same draft value.
actionableExamples: Add a concrete example of callback-up, props-down flow with a shared parent.
followUpConfusion: Add one line distinguishing one-way flow from merely passing props through many layers.

competitors.label: Prop Immutability
competitors.selectionReason: This style-guide rule is a strong tutorial reference because it links one-way data flow to immutable props and state ownership.
competitors.winReason: The style guide is strong on prop mutation, but our page now wins the broader one-way-flow lens: sibling drift, multi-writer state, and the distinction between ownership and prop drilling are more explicit.
theirStrengths: It gives concrete broken-versus-fixed examples around mutating props.
theirGaps: It is centered on an immutability rule, not the broader one-way-flow mental model.

realWorldUseCases: That discipline prevents multi-writer bugs, makes updates traceable in production, and keeps React’s render/reconciliation model safe to restart or interrupt.
followUpConfusion: In React, it typically shows up as mutating props or sharing mutable objects across components.
Prop drilling can be annoying, but it is a different problem from two-way writes or shared mutable data.

realWorldUseCases: The style guide discusses bugs caused by prop mutation, which is close to the one-way-flow discussion.
actionableExamples: It includes focused broken and fixed code examples around prop mutation.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer about why one-way flow matters beyond props alone: multi-writer state and concurrency-safe rendering.

realWorldUseCases: Add one example where both parent and child can write the same data and state drifts.
actionableExamples: Add a concrete anti-pattern example with a child mutating a shared object then show the setter-based fix.
